blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١)
All praise belongs to Allah to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. To Him belongs all praise in the Hereafter, and He is the All-wise, the All-aware. (1)
يَعْلَمُ مَا يَلِجُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا يَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا وَمَا يَنزِلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا يَعْرُجُ فِيهَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلرَّحِيمُ ٱلْغَفُورُ (٢)
He knows whatever enters into the earth and whatever emerges from it, and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends into it, and He is the All-merciful, the All-forgiving. (2)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَا تَأْتِينَا ٱلسَّاعَةُ ۖ قُلْ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّى لَتَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ لَا يَعْزُبُ عَنْهُ مِثْقَالُ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَآ أَصْغَرُ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْبَرُ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣)
The faithless say, ‘The Hour will not overtake us.’ Say, ‘Yes, indeed it will surely overtake you, by my Lord,’ the Knower of the Unseen; not [even] an atom’s weight escapes Him in the heavens or in the earth, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book, (3)
لِّيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ (٤)
that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds.’ For such there will be forgiveness and a noble provision. (4)
وَٱلَّذِينَ سَعَوْ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا مُعَـٰجِزِينَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مِّن رِّجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ (٥)
But those who contend with Our signs seeking to frustrate [their purpose], for such is a painful punishment due to defilement. (5)
وَيَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (٦)
Those who have been given knowledge see that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth and [that] it guides to the path of the All-mighty, the All-laudable. (6)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ هَلْ نَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ يُنَبِّئُكُمْ إِذَا مُزِّقْتُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ إِنَّكُمْ لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (٧)
The faithless say, ‘Shall we show you a man who will inform you [that] when you have been totally rent to pieces you will indeed have a new creation? (7)
أَفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَم بِهِۦ جِنَّةٌۢ ۗ بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ وَٱلضَّلَـٰلِ ٱلْبَعِيدِ (٨)
Has he fabricated a lie against Allah, or is there a madness in him?’ Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter languish in punishment and extreme error. (8)
أَفَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِن نَّشَأْ نَخْسِفْ بِهِمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ أَوْ نُسْقِطْ عَلَيْهِمْ كِسَفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ (٩)
Have they not regarded that which is before them and that which is behind them of the sky and the earth? If We like, We can make the earth swallow them, or let a fragment from the sky fall on them. There is indeed a sign in that for every penitent servant. (9)

(Commentary)

The Surah discusses the three fundamental principles—namely, monotheism (الوحدانية), prophethood (النبوة), and the resurrection (البعث). It mentions these principles along with the objections and doubts raised by those who deny them, subsequently refuting them through various modes of dismissal, including wisdom (حكمة), goodly exhortation (موعظة), and gracious disputation (مجادلة حسنة). The Surah places greater emphasis on explaining the matter of the resurrection (البعث) — the restoration of life to the dead for final judgment — than on other topics; it mentions it at the very opening of the discourse and then returns to it repeatedly until its conclusion.

It is a Meccan Surah, as evidenced by the objectives of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ "All praise belongs to Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth..." (34:1) etc. The objective is to explain the resurrection (البعث) and the recompense (الجزاء) in a manner free from doubt, by pointing to the proof that silences the opponent. The foundation upon which this stands consists of two matters: First, the universality of His ownership, the Exalted, over everything from every aspect, such that any disposal He intends therein is valid for Him—whether it be originating, providing sustenance, causing death, or restoring to life through resurrection and recompense. Second, the perfection of His knowledge, the Exalted, regarding things from all their aspects—a knowledge (علم) that is never subject to absence (عزوب) or cessation—so that He may restore whomever He wills and recompense them for what He knows of their deeds, whether good or evil.

The first of these two matters is pointed out in the first verse before us, and the second in the second verse. From this, it becomes clear that these two verses serve as a preamble for what is contained in the third and fourth verses.

Thus, His saying: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ "All praise belongs to Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" is a glorification of Him for His ownership that extends over everything, such that He has the right to dispose of everything as He wishes and intends.

And His saying: وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ "and to Him belongs [all] praise in the Hereafter" specifies praise for the Hereafter because the first clause implies praise in this world, as the observed system in the heavens and the earth is a worldly system (نظام دنيوي), as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ وَالسَّماواتُ "The day when the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well]" (14:48).

And His saying: وَهُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْخَبِيرُ "and He is the All-wise, the All-aware" concludes the verse with these two noble names to indicate that His disposal in the system of this world, followed by its succession by the system of the Hereafter, is based on wisdom (الحكمة) and expertise (الخبرة). By His wisdom, He made the Hereafter succeed this world; otherwise, creation would have been in vain (لغو) and futile, and the doer of good would not be distinguished from the doer of evil, as He said: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً "We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them in vain"—until He said—أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ "Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:27-28). And by His expertise, He shall gather them, leaving not one of them behind, and recompense every soul for what it has earned.

The All-aware (الخبير) is one of the Beautiful Names of Allah, derived from expertise (الخبرة) — the detailed knowledge of particulars (الجزئيات); thus, it is more specific than the All-knowing (العليم).

His saying, the Exalted: يَعْلَمُ ما يَلِجُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما يَخْرُجُ مِنْها وَما يَنْزِلُ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَما يَعْرُجُ فِيها "He knows whatever finds its way into the earth and whatever comes out of it, and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends into it" (34:2). Entering (الولوج) is the opposite of exiting, and ascending (العروج) is the opposite of descending. The mention of knowledge regarding entering, exiting, descending, and ascending serves as an allusion (كناية) to His knowledge of the movement of every mover and its actions. The conclusion of the verse with His saying: وَهُوَ الرَّحِيمُ الْغَفُورُ "and He is the All-merciful, the All-forgiving" contains an indication that He possesses a constant mercy and a forgiveness that will reach a people due to their faith.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَأْتِينَا السَّاعَةُ قُلْ بَلى وَرَبِّي لَتَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ "The faithless say, ‘The Hour will not overtake us.’ Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord, it will surely overtake you—the Knower of the Unseen...’" (34:3) etc. He mentions their denial of the coming of the Hour (الساعة), which is the Day of Resurrection. They deny it despite the clarity of His universal ownership and His knowledge of everything. There is no room for doubt regarding its coming given these facts, let alone denying its arrival. Therefore, the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded to respond to their statement by saying: قُلْ بَلى وَرَبِّي لَتَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ "Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord, it will surely overtake you’"—meaning the Hour.

Since the primary reason for their denial was the intermingling of things—including the bodies of the dead—with one another, and the transformation of their forms one after another such that no trace of their original essences remains, making their restoration seemingly impossible without distinguishing one from another, He pointed to the refutation of this by saying: عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْزُبُ "the Knower of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — from whose knowledge not [even]... escapes (لا يَعْزُبُ)" — meaning nothing is lost to His knowledge — مِثْقالُ ذَرَّةٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ "the weight of an atom in the heavens or in the earth."

And His saying: وَلا أَصْغَرُ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْبَرُ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ "nor [anything] smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book" is a generalization of His knowledge to everything. Furthermore, it contains an indication that all things, whatever they may be, have a fixity in a manifest Book (كتاب مبين) that does not change or alter, even if their outward forms have vanished from the page of existence. Some discussion regarding the manifest Book (الكتاب المبين) has already preceded in Surah al-An'am and elsewhere.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ "that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds. For such there will be forgiveness and a noble provision" (34:4). The letter lam in لِيَجْزِيَ "that He may reward" is for causality (التعليل), and it is connected to His saying: لَتَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ "it will surely overtake you." In His saying: لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ "For such there will be forgiveness and a noble provision," there is a kind of correspondence to His previous saying: وَهُوَ الرَّحِيمُ الْغَفُورُ "and He is the All-merciful, the All-forgiving."

The verse explains one of the two reasons for the establishment of the Hour, which is that Allah may recompense those who believe and perform righteous deeds (الصالحات) with forgiveness and a noble provision—which is Paradise and all it contains. The final reason is that which is indicated by His saying: وَالَّذِينَ سَعَوْا فِي آياتِنا مُعاجِزِينَ "But those who strive against Our signs, trying to frustrate them..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ سَعَوْا فِي آياتِنا مُعاجِزِينَ أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مِنْ رِجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ "But those who strive against Our signs, trying to frustrate them, for such there will be a punishment of a painful wrath" (34:5). Striving (السعي) is diligence in walking, and frustrating (المعاجزة) is the exaggeration in attempting to incapacitate (إعجاز). It is also said to mean competing or outrunning. The discourse is based on a metaphor by allusion (استعارة بالكناية), as if the signs (الآيات) are a distance they are traversing with haste to incapacitate Allah and outrun Him. Wrath (الرجز) is like rijs (الرجس), meaning filth; perhaps what is meant by it is the evil deed itself, thus indicating the transformation of the deed into a painful punishment for them, or it being the cause of their punishment. It is also said that al-rijz is the worst of punishments.

The verse contains a subtle rebuke (تعريض) to the disbelievers who persist in denying the resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَرَى الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ هُوَ الْحَقَ "Those who have been given knowledge see that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth..." (34:6). The first relative pronoun is the subject (فاعل) of "see" (يرى), the second relative pronoun is its first object (مفعول), and "the truth" (الحق) is its second object. What is meant by "those who have been given knowledge" are the scholars who know Allah and His signs, and by "that which was sent down to him" is the Quran revealed to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

The sentence وَيَرَى "and [they] see" etc., is a new commencement (استئناف) addressing His previous saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا "The faithless say," or it is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the subject of "disbelieved." The meaning is: Those people say, "The Hour will not come to us," denying it out of ignorance, while those who have knowledge of Allah and His signs see that this Quran revealed to you—which informs that the Hour is coming—is the truth (الحق).

And His saying: وَيَهْدِي إِلى صِراطِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ "and it guides to the path of the All-mighty, the All-laudable" is coordinated with "the truth"; meaning, they see that the Quran guides to the path of One who is All-mighty (العزيز) — the invincible who is never overcome in what He intends — and All-laudable (الحميد) — the one praised for all His actions because, despite His might, He only performs what is beautiful — and He is Allah, the Glorified. In the description of "the All-mighty, the All-laudable," there is a counter-point to how He described them in His saying: الَّذِينَ سَعَوْا فِي آياتِنا مُعاجِزِينَ "those who strive against Our signs, trying to frustrate them."

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هَلْ نَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى رَجُلٍ يُنَبِّئُكُمْ إِذا مُزِّقْتُمْ كُلَ مُمَزَّقٍ إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ "The faithless say, ‘Shall we show you a man who will inform you that when you have been totally torn to pieces, you will indeed have a new creation?’" (34:7). This is a statement from them made in a mocking manner, wherein they identify the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to one another by his assertion of the return (المعاد).

Tearing to pieces (التمزيق) is cutting and dispersing. Their being in a "new creation" refers to their being established within it—that is, the renewal of their creation by bringing them to life after their death and their second existence after their non-existence. His saying: إِذا مُزِّقْتُمْ "when you have been [torn to pieces]" is an adverbial qualifier (ظرف) for His saying: إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ "you will indeed have a new creation."

The meaning is: The disbelievers said to one another by way of mocking the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله for his warning them of the resurrection and recompense: "Shall we point you to a man"—meaning the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—"who informs you and tells you that you will be established in a new creation and existence will be renewed for you when your bodies have been completely dispersed and cut into pieces such that no part of them can be distinguished from another?"

His saying, the Exalted: أَفْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً أَمْ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ "Has he fabricated a lie against Allah, or is there a madness in him?..." (34:8) etc. The interrogation is for expressing wonder (التعجيب), for the assertion of the resurrection of bodies after their annihilation was wondrous to them; no sane person would say such a thing except to deceive people and mislead them to obtain some of what they possess. Otherwise, how could the matter be confused for a sane person? For this reason, they alternated the matter between fabrication (الافتراء) and madness (الجنة) in the interrogation. The meaning is: Is he a sane person who lies against Allah by fabricating the claim of resurrection, or is there a kind of madness in him such that he utters whatever occurs to him without sound thought?

And His saying: بَلِ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ فِي الْعذابِ وَالضَّلالِ الْبَعِيدِ "Rather those who do not believe in the Hereafter are in punishment and extreme error." This is a refutation of their statement and a turning away (إضراب) from the hesitation they expressed through their questioning. Its summary is that this is neither a fabrication against Allah nor is there any madness in him; rather, these disbelievers are established in a punishment that will become manifest to them, and that has distanced them from the truth, so they were in a far-reaching delusion (ضلال بعيد) such that they cannot comprehend the truth or submit to it.

The use of the relative pronoun in place of a personal pronoun in His saying: بَلِ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ "Rather those who do not believe in the Hereafter" is to indicate that the cause of their falling into the punishment and delusion they are in is their lack of faith in the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلى ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَما خَلْفَهُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنْ نَشَأْ نَخْسِفْ بِهِمُ الْأَرْضَ أَوْ نُسْقِطْ عَلَيْهِمْ كِسَفاً مِنَ السَّماءِ "Have they not let their sight travel to what is before them and what is behind them of the heaven and the earth? If We like, We can make the earth swallow them, or let fall on them fragments from the sky..." (34:9) etc. This is an exhortation and a warning to them, highlighting the gravity of what they dared to do in denying the signs of Allah and mocking His Messenger. What is meant by His saying: ما بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَما خَلْفَهُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ "what is before them and what is behind them of the heaven and the earth" is the encompassment of the heaven and the earth around them from before them and behind them; wherever they look, they find a heaven shading them and an earth carrying them, with no escape from either.

And His saying: إِنْ نَشَأْ نَخْسِفْ بِهِمُ الْأَرْضَ أَوْ نُسْقِطْ عَلَيْهِمْ كِسَفاً مِنَ السَّماءِ "If We like, We can make the earth swallow them, or let fall on them fragments from the sky" means: since the earth and the heaven encompass them, and both are governed by Our management and are submissive and subservient to Us, if We wish, We could cause the earth to swallow them and destroy them, or drop a piece (كسفاً) of the sky upon them and destroy them. So why do they not desist from such talk?

And His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُنِيبٍ "There is indeed a sign in that for every penitent servant." That is, in what has been mentioned of the encompassment of the heaven and the earth and their being governed by Allah, the Glorified—such that if He wills, He could cause the earth to swallow them or drop fragments of the sky upon them—there is indeed a sign for every penitent servant (عبد منيب) who returns to his Lord in obedience. Such people do not belittle these matters nor do they dare to deny these signs; they only do so because they are arrogant and rebellious, not desiring to turn back (إنابة) to their Lord or return to His obedience.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ مِنَّا فَضْلًا ۖ يَـٰجِبَالُ أَوِّبِى مَعَهُۥ وَٱلطَّيْرَ ۖ وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ ٱلْحَدِيدَ (١٠)
Certainly We gave David our grace: ‘O mountains and birds, chime in with him!’ And We made iron soft for him, (10)
أَنِ ٱعْمَلْ سَـٰبِغَـٰتٍ وَقَدِّرْ فِى ٱلسَّرْدِ ۖ وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا ۖ إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١١)
saying, ‘Make easy coats of mail, and keep the measure in arranging [the links], and act righteously. Indeed I watch what you do.’ (11)
وَلِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ٱلرِّيحَ غُدُوُّهَا شَهْرٌ وَرَوَاحُهَا شَهْرٌ ۖ وَأَسَلْنَا لَهُۥ عَيْنَ ٱلْقِطْرِ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ مَن يَعْمَلُ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ ۖ وَمَن يَزِغْ مِنْهُمْ عَنْ أَمْرِنَا نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١٢)
And for Solomon [We subjected] the wind: its morning course was a month’s journey and its evening course was a month’s journey. We made a fount of [molten] copper flow for him, and [We placed at his service] some of the jinn who would work for him by the permission of his Lord, and if any of them swerved from Our command, We would make him taste the punishment of the Blaze. (12)
يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُۥ مَا يَشَآءُ مِن مَّحَـٰرِيبَ وَتَمَـٰثِيلَ وَجِفَانٍ كَٱلْجَوَابِ وَقُدُورٍ رَّاسِيَـٰتٍ ۚ ٱعْمَلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ دَاوُۥدَ شُكْرًا ۚ وَقَلِيلٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِىَ ٱلشَّكُورُ (١٣)
They built for him as many temples as he wished, and figures, basins like cisterns, and caldrons fixed [in the ground]. ‘O House of David, act thankfully, and few of My servants are grateful.’ (13)
فَلَمَّا قَضَيْنَا عَلَيْهِ ٱلْمَوْتَ مَا دَلَّهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَوْتِهِۦٓ إِلَّا دَآبَّةُ ٱلْأَرْضِ تَأْكُلُ مِنسَأَتَهُۥ ۖ فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ ٱلْجِنُّ أَن لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْغَيْبَ مَا لَبِثُوا۟ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْمُهِينِ (١٤)
When We decreed death for him, nothing apprised them of his death except a worm which gnawed away at his staff. And when he fell down, [the humans] realized that had the jinn known the Unseen, they would not have remained in a humiliating torment. (14)
لَقَدْ كَانَ لِسَبَإٍ فِى مَسْكَنِهِمْ ءَايَةٌ ۖ جَنَّتَانِ عَن يَمِينٍ وَشِمَالٍ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِ رَبِّكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لَهُۥ ۚ بَلْدَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ وَرَبٌّ غَفُورٌ (١٥)
There was certainly a sign for Sheba in their habitation: two gardens, to the right and to the left. ‘Eat of the provision of your Lord and give Him thanks: a good land and an all-forgiving Lord!’ (15)
فَأَعْرَضُوا۟ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَيْلَ ٱلْعَرِمِ وَبَدَّلْنَـٰهُم بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ ذَوَاتَىْ أُكُلٍ خَمْطٍ وَأَثْلٍ وَشَىْءٍ مِّن سِدْرٍ قَلِيلٍ (١٦)
But they disregarded [the path of Allah], so We unleashed upon them a violent flood and replaced their two gardens with two gardens bearing bitter fruit, tamarisk, and sparse lote trees. (16)
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ وَهَلْ نُجَـٰزِىٓ إِلَّا ٱلْكَفُورَ (١٧)
We requited them with that for their ingratitude. Do We not requite ingrates? (17)
وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقُرَى ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا قُرًى ظَـٰهِرَةً وَقَدَّرْنَا فِيهَا ٱلسَّيْرَ ۖ سِيرُوا۟ فِيهَا لَيَالِىَ وَأَيَّامًا ءَامِنِينَ (١٨)
We had placed between them and the towns which We had blessed hamlets prominent [from the main route], and We had ordained the course through them: ‘Travel through them in safety, night and day.’ (18)
فَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا بَـٰعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفَارِنَا وَظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ وَمَزَّقْنَـٰهُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (١٩)
But they said, ‘Our Lord! Make the stages between our journeys far apart,’ and they wronged themselves. So We turned them into folktales and caused them to disintegrate totally. There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]. (19)
وَلَقَدْ صَدَّقَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ ظَنَّهُۥ فَٱتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٠)
Certainly Iblis had his conjecture come true about them. So they followed him—all except a part of the faithful. (20)
وَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْهَا فِى شَكٍّ ۗ وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ (٢١)
He had no authority over them, but that We may ascertain those who believe in the Hereafter from those who are in doubt about it, and your Lord is watchful over all things. (21)

(Commentary)

The verses point to a portion of the accounts of David and Solomon, as Allah granted them of His bounty (الفضل). He bestowed favor upon David by subjecting the mountains and the birds to him and by softening iron for him. He subjected the wind to Solomon—its morning journey was a month’s distance and its evening journey was a month’s distance—and He subjected the jinn to work for him as he wished, making sanctuaries, statues, and other things. He commanded them both to perform righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) out of gratitude, and they were two very grateful servants.

Then the verses turn to the story of Sheba (سبإ), where He blessed them with two gardens on the right and the left so they might live a life of ease and plenty. However, they were ungrateful for the blessing and turned away from gratitude (الشكر). Consequently, He sent upon them the flood of the dam (سيل العرم) and replaced their two gardens with two inferior ones. Their land had been flourishing, but they disbelieved, so He made them mere tales and scattered them utterly. All of this was due to their ingratitude for the blessing and their turning away from thankfulness; and none are so requited except the ungrateful.

The connection of these stories to the preceding discourse on the Resurrection (البعث) is that Allah is the Disposer of the affairs of His servants, and they are immersed in the varieties of His blessings. It is the duty of the one blessed to show gratitude to the Bestower for His blessing, and it is for the Bestower to distinguish between those who are grateful for His blessing and those who disbelieve in it. Since there is no such distinction in this worldly life (النشأة), there must be another life in which the two groups are distinguished; thus, the Resurrection is inevitable.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا داوُدَ مِنَّا فَضْلاً يا جِبالُ أَوِّبِي مَعَهُ وَالطَّيْرَ وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ الْحَدِيدَ — "Certainly We gave David a grace from Us: ‘O mountains, echo [the praises of Allah] along with him, and [you too] O birds!’ And We softened iron for him" (34:10). Bounty (الفضل) refers to a gift (العطية). Echoing (التأويب) is the repeating of sound, derived from al-awb (الأوب) meaning "to return." What is meant here is the echoing of the voice in glorification (التسبيح), as evidenced by His saying regarding this in another place: إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِشْراقِ وَالطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً كُلٌّ لَهُ أَوَّابٌ — "Indeed We disposed the mountains to celebrate [Our] glory with him at sunset and sunrise, and the birds as well, huddled together, each echoing him" (38:18-19). "The birds" (والطير) is conjoined to the grammatical position of "the mountains." From this, the error of those who claim that al-awb (الأوب) means "traveling"—and that the mountains used to travel with him wherever he went—becomes apparent.

His saying: يا جِبالُ أَوِّبِي مَعَهُ وَالطَّيْرَ is an explanation of the bounty given to David. The address directed to the mountains and birds, by which they were subjected, is placed in the position of the subjection itself, which is the gift. This is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect. The meaning is: We subjected the mountains to him to echo with him, as well as the birds. This is what is understood from the subjection of the mountains and birds to him, as indicated by His saying: إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِشْراقِ وَالطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً كُلٌّ لَهُ أَوَّابٌ (38:18-19). His saying: وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ الْحَدِيدَ means: We made it soft for him despite its inherent hardness.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنِ اعْمَلْ سابِغاتٍ وَقَدِّرْ فِي السَّرْدِ — "[Saying], ‘Make ample coats of mail, and measure the links...’" (34:11). Ample (السابغات) is the plural of sabighah (سابغة), which is a wide coat of mail. Weaving (السرد) is the crafting of the coat of mail, and "measuring" it (تقديره) means moderation in it such that its links are proportionate. That is: make wide coats of mail and make their links proportionate. The clause "Make..." (أَنِ اعْمَلْ) is a type of explanation for why the iron was softened for him.

His saying: وَاعْمَلُوا صالِحاً إِنِّي بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ — "...and act righteously. Indeed I watch what you do" (34:11). The meaning of the sentence in itself is clear. Because it occurs in the context of mentioning the granting of bounty and the enumeration of blessings, it conveys the command of gratitude (الشكر), as if it were said: "And We said: Be grateful for the blessings, you and your people, through righteous deeds (العمل الصالح)."

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ غُدُوُّها شَهْرٌ وَرَواحُها شَهْرٌ — "And for Solomon [We subjected] the wind: its morning course was a month’s journey and its evening course was a month’s journey..." (34:12). That is: We subjected the wind to Solomon; the morning journey (غدو) of that wind—which is from the start of the day until noon—was a month's distance, and the evening journey (رواح) of that wind—which is from noon until the end of the day—was a month's distance. This means it would travel in one day the distance of a two-month journey.

His saying: وَأَسَلْنا لَهُ عَيْنَ الْقِطْرِ — "...and We made a fount of molten copper flow for him..." (34:12). Making flow (الإسالة) is the causative form of flowing (السيلان), meaning to run. Copper (القطر) refers to brass or copper. That is: We melted the copper for him so it flowed like a running spring.

His saying: وَمِنَ الْجِنِّ مَنْ يَعْمَلُ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ — "...and some of the jinn who worked for him by the permission of his Lord..." (34:12). That is: a group of jinn—as evidenced by the subsequent "they worked for him" (يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ)—worked before him by the permission of his Lord, subjected to him. وَمَنْ يَزِغْ — "And such of them as swerved..." (34:12), meaning deviated, عَنْ أَمْرِنا — "...from Our command..." (34:12) and did not obey Solomon, نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذابِ السَّعِيرِ — "...We shall make him taste the punishment of the Blaze" (34:12). The apparent context suggests this refers to the punishment of fire in this world rather than the hereafter. The wording of the verse indicates that those subjected to him were some of the jinn, not all of them.

His saying, the Exalted: يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ ما يَشاءُ مِنْ مَحارِيبَ وَتَماثِيلَ وَجِفانٍ كَالْجَوابِ وَقُدُورٍ راسِياتٍ — "They made for him whatever he wished of palaces and statues, basins like water troughs and fixed cooking pots..." (34:13). Sanctuaries (المحاريب) is the plural of mihrab (محراب), which is a place for performing prayer and worship (العبادة). Statues (التماثيل) is the plural of timthal (تمثال), which is a sculpted image of a thing. Basins (الجفان) is the plural of jafnah (جفنة), which is a large food platter. Troughs (الجوابي) is the plural of jabiyah (جابية), a reservoir in which water is collected (يجبى). Pots (القدور) is the plural of qidr (قدر), which is a vessel in which food is cooked. Fixed (الراسيات) means firm; the pots being "fixed" means they were stationary in their places, never moved due to their immense size.

His saying: اعْمَلُوا آلَ داوُدَ شُكْراً — "...‘O House of David, act gratefully!’" (34:13) is an address to Solomon and the rest of those with him from the House of David to act and worship Allah in gratitude to Him. His saying: وَقَلِيلٌ مِنْ عِبادِيَ الشَّكُورُ — "...And few of My servants are grateful" (34:13). The "very grateful" (الشكور) is one who thanks Allah time after time. This sentence is either in the position of exalting the station of the people of gratitude—by indicating that those established in this station are few and are the elite among people—or it is in the position of providing a reason, as if it were said: "They are few, so increase their number."

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا قَضَيْنا عَلَيْهِ الْمَوْتَ ما دَلَّهُمْ عَلى مَوْتِهِ إِلَّا دَابَّةُ الْأَرْضِ تَأْكُلُ مِنْسَأَتَهُ — "When We decreed death for him, nothing informed them of his death except a creature of the earth that ate away at his staff..." (34:14). The "creature of the earth" (دابة الأرض) refers to the termite, according to what has been narrated in reports. The staff (المنسأة) is the walking stick. His saying: فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ الْجِنُّ أَنْ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ الْغَيْبَ ما لَبِثُوا فِي الْعذابِ الْمُهِينِ — "...So when he fell down, the jinn realized that had they known the Unseen, they would not have remained in a humiliating punishment" (34:14). Falling (الخرور) is to drop to the ground.

It is inferred from the context that when he (peace be upon him) was taken, he was leaning on his staff. He remained in that state, standing and leaning on his staff for a period of time, while neither human nor jinn knew of his death. Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent a termite which began to eat his staff until, when it was consumed, the staff broke and Solomon fell to the ground. At that point, they realized he had died, and it became clear to the jinn that had they known the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — they would have known of Solomon's death which was hidden from them, and they would not have remained for this duration of time—from the moment of his passing until his fall—in the humiliating punishment (العذاب المهين) that debased them.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كانَ لِسَبَإٍ فِي مَسْكَنِهِمْ آيَةٌ جَنَّتانِ عَنْ يَمِينٍ وَشِمالٍ — "There was certainly a sign for Sheba in their habitation: two gardens, to the right and to the left..." (34:15). Sheba (سبأ) were the original Arabs in Yemen; they were named—as has been said—after their forefather, Sheba b. Yashjub b. Ya’rub b. Qahtan. His saying: عَنْ يَمِينٍ وَشِمالٍ means to the right and left of their habitation.

His saying: كُلُوا مِنْ رِزْقِ رَبِّكُمْ — "...‘Eat of the provision of your Lord...’" (34:15) is a command to eat from the two gardens, which is a metaphor for their provision from them. Then [they were commanded] to be grateful to Him for His blessing and provision. His saying: بَلْدَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ وَرَبٌّ غَفُورٌ — "...A good land and a forgiving Lord!’" (34:15). That is: a pleasant land suitable for residence, and a Lord who is oft-forgiving, who does not take you to task for your misdeeds.

His saying, the Exalted: فأَعْرَضُوا فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ سَيْلَ الْعَرِمِ وَبَدَّلْناهُمْ بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ ذَواتَيْ أُكُلٍ خَمْطٍ وَأَثْلٍ وَشَيْءٍ مِنْ سِدْرٍ قَلِيلٍ — "But they turned away [in ungratefulness]. So We unleashed upon them the flood of the dam, and replaced their two gardens with two gardens bearing bitter fruit, tamarisk, and a few lote trees" (34:16). The dam (العرم) is the embankment that holds back water; it is also said to mean heavy rain, among other interpretations. Fruit (الأكل) refers to every edible fruit. The bitter fruit (الخمط) is—as has been said—every plant that has taken on a taste of bitterness. The tamarisk (الأثل) is the tarfa’ tree; it is said to be a tree similar to it but larger and without fruit. The lote tree (السدر) is well-known. "Tamarisk" and "a few [lote trees]" are conjoined to "fruit" (أكل), not to "bitter fruit" (خمط).

The meaning is: they—the people of Sheba—turned away from the gratitude they were commanded to show, so We requited them and sent upon them the flood of the dam. It submerged their lands and took away their two gardens, and We replaced their two gardens with two gardens possessing bitter fruit, tamarisks, and a small amount of lote trees.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكَ جَزَيْناهُمْ بِما كَفَرُوا وَهَلْ نُجازِي إِلَّا الْكَفُورَ — "We requited them thus because of their ingratitude. Do We requite any but the ungrateful?" (34:17). "That" (ذلك) refers to what was mentioned of sending the flood and replacing the two gardens; its grammatical position is the second object of "We requited" (جزيناهم). The difference between al-jaza’ (الجزاء) and al-mujazah (المجازاة) is—as has been said—that al-mujazah is only used for evil, while al-jaza’ is more general.

The meaning is: We requited Sheba with that punishment because of their disbelief (كفر) and their turning away from gratitude—or in exchange for that—and We do not requite with evil except those who are intensely ungrateful for the blessings of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ الْقُرَى الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها قُرىً ظاهِرَةً — "We placed between them and the towns We had blessed, prominent towns..." (34:18). The pronoun in "between them" (بينهم) refers to Sheba. The discourse is intended to explain the remainder of their story that was meant to be mentioned. It is conjoined to His saying: "There was... for Sheba" (كانَ لِسَبَإٍ). The "towns We had blessed" (القرى التي باركنا فيها) refers to the towns of Syria (الشام). The towns being "prominent" (ظاهرة) means they were close together, such that one could be seen from the other.

His saying: وَقَدَّرْنا فِيهَا السَّيْرَ — "...and We ordained the stages of travel between them..." (34:18). That is: We made the travel within them according to a measured, proportionate, and consistent ratio; the distance between one town and the next was like the distance between that one and the one following it. His saying: سِيرُوا فِيها لَيالِيَ وَأَيَّاماً آمِنِينَ — "...‘Travel through them by night and day, in safety’" (34:18) assumes a command, meaning: We said, "Travel through these towns in safety if you wish by night and if you wish by day." The meaning is that We established security within them; they could travel through them whenever they wished without fear or anxiety.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقالُوا رَبَّنا باعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفارِنا وَظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ — "But they said, ‘Our Lord! Make the distance between our journeys longer,’ and they wronged themselves..." (34:19). That is: We bestowed upon them what We bestowed of abundant fruits, proximity of dwellings, security of roads, ease of travel, and a life of plenty. But they grew bored and weary of that and said: "Our Lord! Make the distance between our journeys longer," meaning: make our journeys possess long distances in which we must ride mounts and cross deserts and wildernesses. This was transgression (بغي) and ingratitude (كفران) on their part, just as the Children of Israel requested garlic and onions in place of manna and quails.

In short, Allah perfected His blessings upon them in travel through the proximity of dwellings, security of roads, and abundance of bounty, just as He perfected His blessings upon them while they were settled. He desired from them gratitude for that, but they were ungrateful for His blessings in travel just as they were ungrateful for them while settled. So Allah was quick to grant what they had suggested; He destroyed their lands, dispersed their gathering, and scattered their unity.

Thus, His saying: فَقالُوا رَبَّنا باعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفارِنا was an implicit request for the destruction of their lands. His saying: وَظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ means through acts of disobedience (المعاصي).

His saying: فَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَحادِيثَ وَمَزَّقْناهُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ — "...So We made them [the subject of] tales and scattered them utterly" (34:19). That is: We removed their persons and their traces, so nothing remained of them except tales that are told among other stories. They became names without any reality (مسمى) except in the imagination of the imaginer and the fancy of the fancier. We separated them with every possible separation, such that no two parts of their existence remained together but that We separated them. They became like scattered dust without a form, after having been a community of strength and might, until the proverb was struck regarding them: "They scattered like the hands of Sheba."

His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ — "...There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]" (34:19). That is: in this which has been mentioned of their story, there are signs for everyone whose patience (صبر) is great for the sake of Allah and whose gratitude (شكر) is great for His innumerable blessings. One deduces from these signs that it is the duty of man to worship his Lord in gratitude for His blessings, and that behind him is a Day in which he will be resurrected and requited for his deeds.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ صَدَّقَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ ظَنَّهُ فَاتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقاً مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "Iblis certainly found his expectation of them to be true: so they followed him, except a group of the faithful" (34:20). That is: Iblis realized his suspicion regarding them, or he found his suspicion of them to be true, when he said to his Lord: "I will surely lead them all astray" and "You will not find most of them grateful." His saying: فَاتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقاً مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ is an explanation of his suspicion being proven true.

From this, it becomes clear that the plural pronoun in "against them" (عَلَيْهِمْ) here, as well as in the following verse, refers to people in general, not to Sheba specifically, even if the verse applies to them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ لَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِالْآخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْها فِي شَكٍ — "He had no authority over them, but that We might distinguish those who believe in the Hereafter from those who are in doubt about it..." (34:21). The apparent context is that they did not follow him due to any authority (سلطان) he had over them that compelled them to follow him—such that they would be excused—rather, they followed him out of their own evil choice. They choose to follow him, and thus he gains authority over them; it is not that he has authority and then they follow him. The Exalted said: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (15:42). And He said, narrating from Iblis on the Day of Resurrection: وَما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي فَلا تَلُومُونِي وَلُومُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ — "...I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me. So do not blame me, but blame yourselves..." (14:22).

The origin of their following him is a misgiving and doubt (شك) in their hearts regarding the Hereafter, which becomes manifest through its effect, which is the following of Iblis. Thus, His permission for Iblis to have authority over them through their own choice—this degree of authority—is so that the people of doubt in the Hereafter may be distinguished from the people of faith (الإيمان) in it. This does not remove their responsibility in following him, because it stems from their own choice.

So His saying: وَما كانَ لَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ is a negation of all authority. His saying: إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ — meaning "so that We may distinguish" — مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِالْآخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْها فِي شَكٍ is an exception for his authority over them through the path of their following him by their own choice. The goal has been placed in the position of that which leads to the goal; that is, the aforementioned distinction is placed in the position of the authority gained through voluntary following.

The restriction of faith and doubt to the Hereafter (الآخرة) in the verse is because the only deterrent from disobedience and the only motivator toward obedience is faith in the Hereafter, rather than faith in Allah and His Messenger if there were no Hereafter. As the Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ — "...Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26). His saying: وَرَبُّكَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ — "...And your Lord is watchful over all things" (34:21). That is: He is All-Knowing with a knowledge that no object of knowledge escapes through forgetfulness, oversight, or otherwise. In this is a warning against ingratitude and disobedience, and a threat to the people of disbelief and sin.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Kamal al-Din, with its chain of transmission to Hisham b. Salim, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): In a narration in which he mentions the story of David (peace be upon him), he said: "He went out reciting the Psalms (الزبور), and whenever he recited the Psalms, no mountain, stone, or bird remained but that it responded to him."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: أَنِ اعْمَلْ سابِغاتٍ, he said: "Coats of mail." وَقَدِّرْ فِي السَّرْدِ, he said: "The rivets that are in the links." Regarding the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: وَلِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ غُدُوُّها شَهْرٌ وَرَواحُها شَهْرٌ, he said: "The wind used to carry the throne of Solomon, traveling with it in the morning a month's journey and in the evening a month's journey."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Dawud b. al-Husayn and from Aban b. ‘Uthman, from al-Fadl Abi al-’Abbas, who said: I said to Abu Ja’far (peace be upon him): يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ ما يَشاءُ مِنْ مَحارِيبَ وَتَماثِيلَ وَجِفانٍ كَالْجَوابِ. He said: "They were not statues of men and women, but rather statues of trees and the like."

In the same source, from some of our companions in a marfu’ report from Hisham b. al-Hakam, who said: Abu al-Hasan Musa b. Ja’far (peace be upon him) said: "O Hisham, then Allah praised the few, saying: وَقَلِيلٌ مِنْ عِبادِيَ الشَّكُورُ."

I say: This meaning has occurred in several narrations, and it corresponds to one of the two meanings mentioned previously at the end of the verse.

In al-’Ilal, with its chain of transmission from Abu Ja’far (peace be upon him), who said: "Solomon b. David commanded the jinn, and they fashioned for him a pavilion of glass. While he was leaning on his staff in the pavilion, watching the jinn and how they were looking at him, he happened to turn around and saw a man with him in the pavilion. He said to him: 'Who are you?' He replied: 'I am the one who accepts no bribes and fears no kings; I am the Angel of Death.' So he took his soul while he was standing, leaning on his staff in the pavilion, and the jinn were looking at him."

He said: "They remained for a year working hard for him until Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent the termite, which ate his staff (المنسأة). When he fell, the jinn realized that had they known the Unseen, they would not have remained in the humiliating punishment."

I say: His (peace be upon him) remaining in the state of standing, leaning on his staff for a year, is mentioned in several narrations of both the Shi’a and the Sunnis.

In al-Majma’, in a narration from Farwah b. Musayk, who said: I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about Sheba: "Was it a man or a woman?" He said: "He was a man from the Arabs. He fathered ten; six of them went to the right (Yemen) and four went to the left (Syria). As for those who went to the right, they were al-Azd, Kindah, Madhhij, al-Ash’arun, Anmar, and Himyar." A man from the people asked: "What is Anmar?" He said: "Those from whom are Khath’am and Bajilah. As for those who went to the left, they were ‘Amilah, Judham, Lakhm, and Ghassan."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several compilers of the Jami’ and Sunan works from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). What is meant by "going to the right" (التيامن) and "going to the left" (التشاؤم) is settling in Yemen and Syria (al-Sham).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Sudayr, who said: A man asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: فَقالُوا رَبَّنا باعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفارِنا وَظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ. He said: "These were a people who had contiguous towns, such that they could see one another, with running rivers and manifest wealth. But they were ungrateful for the blessings of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and they changed the well-being Allah had granted them. So Allah changed the blessing they had—and Allah does not change what is with a people until they change what is in themselves. Thus, Allah sent upon them the flood of the dam, which dispersed their towns, destroyed their homes, and took away their wealth. He replaced their gardens with two gardens bearing bitter fruit, tamarisk, and a few lote trees." Then He said: ذلِكَ جَزَيْناهُمْ بِما كَفَرُوا وَهَلْ نُجازِي إِلَّا الْكَفُورَ.

I say: It has been mentioned in several narrations that the "towns We had blessed" are the People of the House (أهل البيت) of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the "prominent towns" are the intermediaries between them and the people, such as the carriers of their narrations and others. This belongs to the inner depth (بطن) of the Quran and is not part of the formal exegesis (التفسير) at all.

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا لَهُمْ فِيهِمَا مِن شِرْكٍ وَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْهُم مِّن ظَهِيرٍ (٢٢)
Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim [to be gods] besides Allah! They do not control [even] an atom’s weight in the heavens or the earth, nor do they have any share in [either of] them, nor is any of them His helper.’ (22)
وَلَا تَنفَعُ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُۥ ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فُزِّعَ عَن قُلُوبِهِمْ قَالُوا۟ مَاذَا قَالَ رَبُّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (٢٣)
Intercession is of no avail with Him, except for those whom He permits. When fear is lifted from their hearts, they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth, and He is the All-exalted, the All-great.’ (23)
۞ قُلْ مَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَإِنَّآ أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى أَوْ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٤)
Say, ‘Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘Allah! Indeed either we or you are rightly guided or in manifest error.’ (24)
قُل لَّا تُسْـَٔلُونَ عَمَّآ أَجْرَمْنَا وَلَا نُسْـَٔلُ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٥)
Say, ‘You will not be questioned about our guilt, nor shall we be questioned about what you do.’ (25)
قُلْ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا رَبُّنَا ثُمَّ يَفْتَحُ بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُوَ ٱلْفَتَّاحُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٢٦)
Say, ‘Our Lord will bring us together, then He will judge between us with justice, and He is the All-knowing Judge.’ (26)
قُلْ أَرُونِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَلْحَقْتُم بِهِۦ شُرَكَآءَ ۖ كَلَّا ۚ بَلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٧)
Say, ‘Show me those whom you associate with Him as partners.’ No! [They can never show any such partner]. Indeed, He is Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (27)
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا كَآفَّةً لِّلنَّاسِ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٨)
We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and warner to all mankind, but most people do not know. (28)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٩)
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (29)
قُل لَّكُم مِّيعَادُ يَوْمٍ لَّا تَسْتَـْٔخِرُونَ عَنْهُ سَاعَةً وَلَا تَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٣٠)
Say, ‘Your promised hour is a day that you shall neither defer nor advance by an hour.’ (30)

(Commentary)

These verses establish monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and present arguments surrounding it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ ادْعُوا الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ لا يَمْلِكُونَ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ "Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim [to be gods] besides Allah: they do not control [even] the weight of an atom..." (34:22) until the end of the verse. He commanded the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to argue for the invalidity of the divinity of their gods based on their inability to respond to supplication. Thus, His saying: قُلِ ادْعُوا الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ "Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim besides Allah’" means: invoke those whom you claimed to be gods besides Allah—the two objects of the verb "you claimed" (زَعَمْتُمْ) are omitted because the context indicates them—and their "invoking" (دعاؤهم) refers to asking them for any of their needs.

His saying: لا يَمْلِكُونَ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ "they do not control [even] the weight of an atom in the heavens or the earth" stands in the position of a response, as if it were asked: "What happens if they invoke them?" It is replied: "They do not respond to them with anything because they 'do not control [even] the weight of an atom in the heavens or the earth.'" Had they possessed anything, they would have responded. Lordship (الربوبية) and divinity (الألوهية) are only realized if the Lord and God possesses something that the human being needs, so He may grant it to him and bestow it upon him, thereby deserving worship (العبادة) in return as gratitude, and thus be worshipped. However, if He possesses nothing, He is neither a Lord nor a God.

His saying: وَما لَهُمْ فِيهِما مِنْ شِرْكٍ "nor do they have any share in them": the ownership denied in the previous sentence—"they do not control" (لا يَمْلِكُونَ) etc.—was absolute ownership (الملك المطلق) extending over everything. The ownership denied in this sentence is limited, partial ownership (الملك المحدود المتبعض) that extends over some things but not all, whether jointly or separately. However, the polytheists did not claim a joint ownership between their gods and Allah, the Glorified, in a shared sense; rather, they claimed that each of their gods owned a specific type of creation or a portion of it, while Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of lords and the God of gods.

Based on this, it would have been mandatory for their gods to respond when invoked regarding that portion of creation they supposedly own. Their failure to respond is a disclosure of the absence of their lordship and divinity.

His saying: وَما لَهُ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ ظَهِيرٍ "nor is there any backer for Him from among them": meaning there is no helper (معين) for Allah, the Glorified, from among them—neither all of them nor some of them—who assists Him in a matter where His inability to manage (تدبير) is assumed. For if there were a backer (ظهير) for Him among them who assisted Him in management, that backer would be an owner and would respond when invoked regarding that which he backs; since he does not, he is not.

It becomes clear from the preceding that the argument of the verse—denying ownership by the absence of their response to the supplication of the invoker—applies to all three scenarios: their absolute ownership of what is in the heavens and the earth, their ownership by way of partnership with Allah, the Glorified, and they or some of them being a backer for Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَنْفَعُ الشَّفاعَةُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ "Intercession is of no avail with Him except for those whom He permits." (34:23). The polytheists used to believe in the intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — of their gods, as Allah, the Glorified, recounted from them in His saying: هؤُلاءِ شُفَعاؤُنا عِنْدَ اللهِ "These are our intercessors with Allah" (10:18).

Their intent by "intercession" was not the intercession of the Day of Resurrection which the Noble Quran establishes—for they did not believe in the Return (المعاد)—but rather intercession in this world for their worshippers before Allah, the Glorified, so that He might grant them success by fulfilling their needs and rectifying their affairs through the mediation of their gods.

Since the gods are creatures of Allah and owned by Him in every respect, they do not possess intercession of their own accord independently, unless Allah, the Glorified, grants them that ownership, which is His "permission" (الإذن) for them to intercede. Thus, the basis of their intercession—if they were to intercede—would be by the permission of Allah, the Glorified.

Regarding His saying: إِلَّا لِمَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ "except for those whom He permits", it is possible that the preposition lām in "for those" (لِمَنْ) is the lām of possession, and the one intended by "whom He permits" is the intercessor (الشافع) from among the angels. The meaning would be: intercession is of no avail unless the intercessor possesses it through permission from Allah. It is also possible that it is the lām of causality, and the one intended by "whom He permits" is the one interceded for (المشفوع له). The meaning would then be: intercession is of no avail except for the sake of those among the interceded-for whom He has permitted. The author of al-Kashshaf said: "This—meaning the second view—is a subtle view, and it is the [correct] view." End quote.

And it is indeed the [correct] view, for the angels—as understood from His saying, the Exalted—are intermediaries (وسائط) for the execution and implementation of the Divine command. He, the Exalted, said: لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ "They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command" (21:27), and He said: جاعِلِ الْمَلائِكَةِ رُسُلاً أُولِي أَجْنِحَةٍ "who made the angels messengers, possessing wings" (35:1). This aforementioned intermediation is a form of intercession, as previously discussed in the chapters on intercession in the first volume of this book.

Thus, all angels are intercessors, but not in every matter nor for everyone; rather, in a matter Allah has permitted and for those He has permitted. Therefore, the denial of their intercession except with permission is more suited to the interceded-for than the intercessors. Thus, the verse carries the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى "and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased" (21:28), rather than the meaning of His saying: ما مِنْ شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِنْ بعدِ إِذْنِهِ "there is no intercessor except after His leave" (10:3).

His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا فُزِّعَ عَنْ قُلُوبِهِمْ قالُوا ما ذا قالَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ "When fear is lifted from their hearts, they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth, and He is the All-exalted, the All-great.’" (34:23). "Lifting fear" (التفزيع) is the removal and uncovering of terror. The plural pronouns—as indicated by the context—refer to the intercessors, who are the angels.

The implication of His saying: حَتَّى إِذا فُزِّعَ عَنْ قُلُوبِهِمْ "When fear is lifted from their hearts"—which is a terminal point (غاية)—is that there must be a preceding state limited by it, which is that their hearts are in a state of prolonged fear (فزع) awaiting the command of Allah, the Glorified, until it is lifted by the issuance of the command from Him. Thus, the verse carries the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ "To Allah prostrates..."—until He said— وَالْمَلائِكَةُ وَهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ "and the angels [too], and they are not arrogant. They fear their Lord [who is] above them, and they do what they are commanded" (16:49-50). Fear (الفزع) is the being affected and the contraction resulting from dread, and this is what is intended by their prostration in humility out of fear of their Lord above them.

By this, it becomes clear that the meaning of their fear until it is lifted from them is that humility (التذلل) has enveloped their hearts—specifically their humility in their capacity as causes (أسباب) and intercessors for the penetration of Divine commands and their occurrence exactly as issued and intended. The uncovering of this humility is their reception of the Divine command and their engagement in action, as if nothing of their existence appears except their act and their obedience to Allah in what He commanded them; and that there is no intermediary between Allah, the Glorified, and the act except His command. Understand this well.

Fear and its lifting were attributed to their "hearts" (قلوبهم) to indicate that they are oblivious and turned away from themselves and from everything except their Lord. In this state, they perceive nothing other than Him, until the fear is lifted from their hearts upon the issuance of the Divine command without delay or failure—for the matter is not such that it can be suspended or delayed from occurring. He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is" (36:82). What is understood from the verse, in light of this meaning, is that they are in a state of fear until their fear is removed by the issuance of the Divine command.

His saying: قالُوا ما ذا قالَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا الْحَقَ "they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth’" indicates that they are many groups, some asking others about the Divine command after its issuance and the lifting of fear from the hearts of the questioners.

It also becomes clear from this that the lifting of fear and the descent of the command to some of them precedes its descent to others, for the necessity of a question is that the one questioned must be knowledgeable of what is asked before the questioner.

Thus, they have different ranks and varying stations (مقامات), some above others. The lower ranks receive the Divine command from the higher ranks without failure or delay, which constitutes the obedience of the lower among them to the higher. This is also understood by reflecting on His saying, the Exalted: وَما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ "There is none among us but has a known station" (37:164), and His saying in describing the Trustworthy Spirit: ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ "powerful and eminent with the Lord of the Throne, obeyed there and trustworthy" (81:20-21).

So among them is the one obeyed (مطاع) and the one obedient (مطيع), yet there is no obedience in that except to Allah, the Glorified, because the one obeyed among them has no function other than conveying what reached him of the Divine command to the obedient one below him. This can also be understood from the description of the speech as "the truth" (الحق) in His saying: قالُوا ما ذا قالَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا الْحَقَ "they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth’"—meaning He spoke the firm word (القول الثابت) to which there is no path for falsehood or change.

How subtle is the conclusion of the verse with His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ "and He is the All-exalted, the All-great"—meaning He is the Exalted (العلي) below Whom is everything, and the Great (الكبير) beside Whom everything is small. Thus, the noble angels have nothing but to receive His truthful word, comply with it, and obey it as He wills.

It has thus been gathered from the noble verse that the angels are fearful in themselves, humble in their essences, oblivious to everything except their Lord, gazing toward the courtyard of Might and Grandeur in anticipation of the issuance of the command until the fear is lifted from their hearts by the issuance and descent of the command. Despite this, they are different groups with varying stations in terms of highness and lowness, each higher one mediating in conveying the descending command to the one below him.

Thus, despite being intercessors and mediating causes, they do not intercede or mediate in the occurrence of any event among the events of creation and management (التدبير) except by a specific permission from their Lord for its occurrence. They then carry the command descended to them until they realize it in existence, without being independent in anything of themselves or acting on their own opinion. And one whose state is this—perceiving nothing but obedience to his Lord in what He commands him—how could he be an independent Lord in his affair, to whom management is delegated, giving what he wills and withholding what he wills?

There are other various opinions regarding the verse: Among them is that the pronouns in "their hearts" (قُلُوبِهِمْ) and the second "they said" (قالُوا) refer to the polytheists rather than the angels, and the pronoun in the first "they said" (قالُوا) refers to the angels. The meaning would be: until when fear is lifted from the hearts of the polytheists at the time of terror, the angels say to them: "What did your Lord say?" The polytheists say to them: "The truth," thus confessing what they denied in the world.

Among them is that the pronoun in "their hearts" (قُلُوبِهِمْ) refers to the angels, and the meaning is that the angels entrusted with deeds, when they ascend with the deeds of the servants to heaven and they have a loud clamor and sound, the angels fear that it is the Hour. They become terrified and fall down in prostration to Allah, the Glorified, until when the fear is lifted from their hearts and they realize it is not so, they ask: "What did your Lord say?" They say: "The truth."

Among them is that when Allah sent the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله after an interval (فترة) between him and Jesus عليه‌السلام during which no revelation (الوحي) — the divine communication to prophets — had descended, Allah, the Glorified, sent Gabriel with the revelation. When he descended, the angels thought he had descended with something regarding the Hour, so they were struck unconscious by that. Gabriel then began to pass through every heaven and lift the fear from the angels residing therein. They raised their heads and said to one another: "What did your Lord say?" They said: "The truth," meaning the revelation.

Among them is that the pronoun refers to the angels, and the meaning is that when Allah, the Glorified, reveals to some of the angels, the angels are overcome with fainting upon hearing the revelation; they are struck unconscious and fall down in prostration due to the great sign (الآية). When the fear is lifted from their hearts, the angels ask that angel to whom it was revealed: "What did your Lord say?" or they ask one another: "What did your Lord say?" and they realize the matter concerns others.

After reflecting on the noble verse and contemplating what we have presented, you will know the weakness of these opinions, and that none of them—even if assumed correct in itself—is suitable as an interpretation for it.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلِ اللهُ "Say, ‘Who provides for you from the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘Allah.’" (34:24) etc. This is another argument against the polytheists from the perspective of provision (الرزق), which is the primary justification for their taking of gods; for they excuse themselves in worshipping the gods by saying that they please them, so they expand their provision for them and they become happy thereby.

So He commanded the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ask them: who provides for them from the heavens and the earth? The answer to it is that it is Allah, the Glorified, because provision is a creation in itself, and there is no creator—even according to the polytheists—except Allah, exalted is His Name. However, they refrain from confessing it with their tongues even if their hearts acknowledge it. Therefore, he was commanded to represent them in the answer, so He said: قُلِ اللهُ "Say, ‘Allah.’"

His saying: وَإِنَّا أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلى هُدىً أَوْ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ "Indeed either we or you are on guidance or in manifest error" is the completion of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله speech. This statement, after presenting the definitive proof and the clarity of the truth in the matter of divinity, is based on following the path of fairness (الإنصاف). Its meaning is that every statement is either guidance (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — or error (الضلال), with no third option between them in terms of negation and affirmation. We and you are upon two different statements that do not coexist; so either we are upon guidance and you are in error, or you are upon guidance and we are in error. So look with the eye of fairness at the proof presented to you and distinguish the guided from the erring, and the one on truth from the one on falsehood.

The variation in expression in His sayings: لَعَلى هُدىً "on guidance" and فِي ضَلالٍ "in error" using the words "on" (على) and "in" (في)—as has been said—is to indicate that the guided one is as if he is elevated upon a lighthouse, looking out over the path and its goal wherein lies his happiness; while the erring one is submerged in darkness, not knowing where he places his foot, where he is walking, or what is intended for him.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لا تُسْئَلُونَ عَمَّا أَجْرَمْنا وَلا نُسْئَلُ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ "Say, ‘You will not be questioned about our crimes, nor shall we be questioned about what you do.’" (34:25). Meaning that an action, especially an evil action, does not transcend its doer, and its consequence does not attach to anyone but him. Thus, no one else is questioned about it. Therefore, you will not be questioned about our crimes; rather, we are the ones questioned about them. And we will not be questioned about what you do; rather, you are the ones questioned.

This is a preparation for what follows in the next verse regarding the gathering and the "opening" (الفتح), for when two groups differ in deeds, good and evil, it is mandatory that an opening occur between them and each be distinguished from the other, so that the recompense of his deed—whether good or evil, happiness or misery—may attach to him. The one who opens and distinguishes is the Lord, the Exalted.

In expressing their own deeds as "crimes" (إجرام) and on the side of the polytheists saying: تَعْمَلُونَ "what you do" rather than saying "what you commit as crimes," there is an adoption of refined etiquette (حسن الأدب) in debate.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنا رَبُّنا ثُمَّ يَفْتَحُ بَيْنَنا بِالْحَقِّ وَهُوَ الْفَتَّاحُ الْعَلِيمُ "Say, ‘Our Lord will bring us together, then He will judge between us with justice, and He is the All-judge, the All-knowing.’" (34:26). Since it was mandatory that the recompense of his deed attach to both the doer of good and the doer of evil, and its necessity was the distinction between them by gathering then separating, that was the affair of the Manager of the matter, who is the Lord. He commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to remind them that the one who gathers everyone then opens between them with the truth is Allah. He is the Lord of these and those, for He is the All-judge (الفتاح) — the one who decisively separates and judges between things — the All-knowing (العليم). He opens between every two things through creation and management, whereby this thing is distinguished from that thing, as He said: أَنَّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ كانَتا رَتْقاً فَفَتَقْناهُما "that the heavens and the earth were a solid mass, and We clove them asunder" (21:30). And He is the All-knowing of everything.

Thus, the verse establishes the Resurrection (البعث) to distinguish the doer of good from the doer of evil first, then the restriction of distinction and recompense to His side, the Exalted, by the restriction of Lordship to Him, thereby nullifying the lordship of those they took as lords.

Al-Fattah (الفتاح) is among the Beautiful Names of Allah, and "opening" (الفتح) is the creation of a separation between two things for a benefit that follows it, like opening a door for entry by creating a separation between its two leaves. The opening between two things is so that each of them may be distinguished from the other by its essence (الذات), attributes, and acts.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرُونِيَ الَّذِينَ أَلْحَقْتُمْ بِهِ شُرَكاءَ كَلَّا بَلْ هُوَ اللهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ "Say, ‘Show me those whom you have joined with Him as partners.’ No! Rather He is Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise." (34:27). Another command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ask them to show him their gods so he may examine whether they possess the necessary attributes for a God deserving of worship, such as independence in life, knowledge, power, hearing, and sight. This is the meaning of His saying: أَرُونِيَ الَّذِينَ أَلْحَقْتُمْ بِهِ شُرَكاءَ "Show me those whom you have joined with Him as partners"—meaning you joined them to Him as partners for Him.

Then He Himself rebuked [them] and said: كَلَّا "No!"—they cannot be partners to Him. For they either show him the idols in their capacity as objects of worship counted as their gods, which are dead bodies devoid of life, knowledge, and power; or they show him the lords of these idols, who are the angels and others, by making the idols statues pointing to them. Although these are not devoid of life, knowledge, and power, whatever they possess of the attributes of perfection is bestowed upon them by Allah, the Glorified; they have no independence in any of these attributes nor in the acts branching from them. So where is the independence in management (التدبير) that they claim is delegated to them? For the Necessary Existence (الوجود الواجبي) with His infinite perfection prevents there being in His creation anyone who shares with Him in any of His perfection.

Unless they claim that He shared with them some of His functions for the management of His creation without them having an essential suitability (صلاحية ذاتية)—and this contradicts His wisdom (الحكمة), the Exalted.

This argument has been pointed to by His saying: بَلْ هُوَ اللهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ "Rather He is Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise." For His Might (العزة), the Exalted—which is the prevention of His side from being encroached upon by any transgressor into the sanctuary of His perfection because He is not limited by any boundary—prevents anyone other than Him from sharing in any of His attributes of perfection, such as Lordship and Divinity, which terminate in the Essence. This would be the case if the partnership were due to an essential suitability of the partner. If it were due to an arbitrary will from Him without a real suitability from the partner, then Divine Wisdom prevents that.

It has thus become clear that the verse contains a definitive, demonstrative proof (حجة قاطعة برهانية), so reflect well upon it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً لِلنَّاسِ بَشِيراً وَنَذِيراً وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and warner to all mankind, but most people do not know." (34:28). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The kaff (الكف) is the human hand, which is that by which one grasps and spreads. Kaffatuhu (كففته) means I touched his hand. Kaffatuhu [also] means I struck him with the hand and pushed him away with it. Al-Kaff became conventionally used for pushing away (الدفع) in any manner, whether with the hand or otherwise, until it was said: 'a man is makfuf' (مكفوف) for one whose sight is seized. And His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً لِلنَّاسِ 'We did not send you except as a kaffah to mankind' means as one who restrains (كافا) them from sins. The hā’ in it is for emphasis (المبالغة), like their saying: rāwiyah (prolific narrator), ‘allāmah (great scholar), and nassābah (great genealogist)." End quote.

This meaning is supported by his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله being described as a bearer of good news (بشير) and a warner (نذير). Thus, His saying: بَشِيراً وَنَذِيراً "as a bearer of good news and warner" are two circumstantial qualifiers (حالان) explaining his attribute for His saying: كَافَّةً لِلنَّاسِ "as a kaffah to mankind."

It has sometimes been said that the implied structure is: "We did not send you except with an sending that is kaffah (encompassing) for mankind," but this is not free from affectation and far-fetchedness.

As for kaffah being in the sense of "all together" and a circumstantial qualifier for "mankind" (الناس)—meaning: "We did not send you except to mankind all together"—the Basran grammarians prevent the advancement of the circumstantial qualifier before its genitive owner, while the Kufans permit it.

Know that although the literal meaning (منطوق) of the verse pertains to prophethood (النبوة), and in it there is a transition from the discourse on monotheism to the discourse on prophethood similar to the following verses, yet in its implication (مدلول) there is another argument for monotheism. This is because the message (الرسالة) is among the necessities of Lordship, whose function is the management of people on the path to their happiness and their journey toward the goals of their existence. Thus, the universality of his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله message—and he is the Messenger of Allah, the Exalted, not the messenger of anyone else—is a proof that Lordship is restricted to Allah, the Glorified. For if there were a lord other than Him, his messenger would have come to them, and the message of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would not have encompassed them, or it would have encompassed them but they would have needed someone else along with him. This is the meaning of the saying of Ali عليه‌السلام—as narrated—: "If your Lord had a partner, his messengers would have come to you."

This is supported by what is at the end of the verse: وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "but most people do not know." For the indication of the restriction of the message to the messengers of Allah upon the restriction of Lordship to Allah, exalted is His Name, is more closely related to the people's ignorance than his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله being a messenger who restrains them from sins as a bearer of good news and a warner.

The meaning of the verse based on this is: they cannot show you a partner for Him, while the fact is that We have not sent you except as a restrainer for all people, a bearer of good news and a warner. If they had a god other than Us, it would not have been possible for Us to send you to them while they are worshippers of another god. And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ "They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’" (34:29). A question about the time of the gathering and the opening, which is the Resurrection. Thus, the verse is connected to His previous saying: قُلْ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنا رَبُّنا "Say, ‘Our Lord will bring us together...’" the verse. This is also among the evidences for what we previously presented regarding the meaning of His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً "We did not send you except as a kaffah...". Otherwise, this verse and the one after it would be intervening between His saying: وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ "We did not send you..." the verse, and the following verses dealing with the matter of prophethood.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَكُمْ مِيعادُ يَوْمٍ لا تَسْتأْخِرُونَ عَنْهُ ساعَةً وَلا تَسْتَقْدِمُونَ "Say, ‘Your tryst is a day that you shall not put back for an hour, nor shall you bring it forward.’" (34:30). A command from Him, the Exalted, to answer them that they have an appointed time (ميعاد) of a day that is decreed and inevitable, which will not fail to occur; it is certainly happening. The time of its occurrence does not vary at all—meaning that Allah has promised it with a promise He does not break, except that the time of its occurrence is hidden, known to none but Allah, the Glorified.

What has been said—that what is intended by it is the day of death—is not sound, for they only asked about what was previously promised, which is the day of gathering and opening. Gathering then opening are among the characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, not the day of death.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام: regarding His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا فُزِّعَ عَنْ قُلُوبِهِمْ ـ قالُوا ما ذا قالَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ "When fear is lifted from their hearts, they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth, and He is the All-exalted, the All-great.’" He said: "That is because the inhabitants of the heavens did not hear any revelation (وحيا) in the period between the sending of Jesus son of Mary until the sending of Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.
